Apple___Apple_scab.description: Apple scab is a fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis. It appears as dark, scaly lesions on leaves and fruit, reducing yield and quality.
Apple___Apple_scab.pesticide: Captan, Mancozeb, or Myclobutanil fungicides applied preventively before infection.
Apple___Apple_scab.organic_solution: Neem oil spray (2% solution) every 7–10 days. Sulfur-based sprays as a contact fungicide.
prevention: Remove and destroy fallen leaves in autumn
Prune trees to improve air circulation
Apply mulch to reduce soil splash

Apple___Black_rot.description: Black rot is caused by the fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa. It causes circular brown lesions on leaves and black, shriveled mummified fruits.
Apple___Black_rot.pesticide: Captan or Thiophanate-methyl applied every 10–14 days during wet periods.
Apple___Black_rot.organic_solution: Copper-based fungicide spray. Remove and destroy all mummified fruits.
prevention: Prune out dead or infected wood
Remove all mummified fruits from tree and ground

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: Cedar-apple rust is caused by the fungus Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. It requires both apple and cedar trees to complete its life cycle, causing yellow-orange spots on leaves.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.pesticide: Myclobutanil or Propiconazole fungicides applied at bud break.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.organic_solution: Sulfur-based sprays during early spring. Remove nearby cedar/juniper trees if possible.
prevention: Remove nearby eastern red cedar and juniper trees
Apply fungicide preventively at bud break
Monitor orange gelatinous galls on nearby cedars in spring

Apple___healthy.description: The apple leaf appears healthy with no visible signs of disease, pest damage, or nutritional deficiency.
Apple___healthy.pesticide: No treatment needed. Maintain preventive spraying schedule.
Apple___healthy.organic_solution: Continue good cultural practices. Compost application to improve soil health.
prevention: Regular scouting for early disease detection
Maintain balanced nutrition with soil testing

Blueberry___healthy.description: The blueberry plant appears healthy. No disease or pest infestation detected.
Blueberry___healthy.pesticide: No treatment required. Preventive measures are sufficient.
Blueberry___healthy.organic_solution: Maintain acidic soil pH (4.5–5.5) and mulch with wood chips.
prevention: Apply pine bark or sawdust mulch

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on cherry is caused by Podosphaera clandestina. It appears as white powdery coating on young leaves and shoots, stunting growth.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.pesticide: Sulfur, Myclobutanil, or Trifloxystrobin fungicides. Apply at first sign of infection.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.organic_solution: Potassium bicarbonate spray (1 tsp per quart of water). Neem oil spray every 7 days.

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.description: The cherry plant is healthy with no signs of disease.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.organic_solution: Continue regular monitoring and good cultural practices.
prevention: Regular inspection of leaves and fruit

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: Gray leaf spot is caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. It produces rectangular, tan to gray lesions parallel to leaf veins, reducing photosynthesis and yield.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.pesticide: Azoxystrobin, Propiconazole, or Pyraclostrobin fungicides applied at VT (tasseling) stage.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.organic_solution: Copper hydroxide spray. Crop rotation is most effective organic control.
prevention: Reduce tillage to minimize leaf wetness
Ensure proper plant spacing for airflow
Apply fungicide preventively in high-risk areas

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Common rust is caused by Puccinia sorghi. It produces small, circular to elongated powdery orange-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.pesticide: Mancozeb, Propiconazole, or Azoxystrobin applied at early rust appearance.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.organic_solution: Sulfur-based fungicide. Good air circulation reduces severity.
prevention: Plant early in season to avoid late-season high humidity
Monitor fields weekly from V6 stage onwards

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Northern Leaf Blight (NLB) is caused by Exserohilum turcicum. It produces long, cigar-shaped gray-green to tan lesions on leaves, reducing yield significantly.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.pesticide: Azoxystrobin or Propiconazole at V6-VT stage when disease first appears.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.organic_solution: Copper-based fungicide. Remove crop debris after harvest.
prevention: Incorporate crop debris to reduce overwintering spores
Apply fungicide preventively in high-risk regions

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: Corn leaves appear healthy with no signs of fungal, bacterial, or viral disease.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.organic_solution: Compost application and balanced fertilization for continued health.
prevention: Control weeds to reduce disease habitat

Grape___Black_rot.description: Black rot in grapes is caused by the fungus Guignardia bidwellii. It causes circular brown lesions on leaves and shriveled black 'mummified' berries.
Grape___Black_rot.pesticide: Myclobutanil or Mancozeb applied from early shoot growth through veraison.
Grape___Black_rot.organic_solution: Copper fungicide sprays every 10–14 days. Remove mummified berries promptly.
prevention: Remove and destroy mummified fruits and infected shoots
Apply preventive sprays before rain events

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: Esca is a complex wood disease caused by multiple fungi. Symptoms include interveinal leaf scorching (tiger-stripe pattern) and dark berries with internal browning.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).pesticide: No curative chemical control available. Arsenite of sodium (banned in many countries) was historically used.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).organic_solution: Remove and destroy infected wood. Protect pruning wounds with wound sealant or Trichoderma-based biocontrol.
prevention: Apply wound protectant (Trichoderma) immediately after pruning
Remove wood showing internal brown staining
Avoid water stress and excessive nitrogen

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: Isariopsis leaf spot is caused by Isariopsis clavispora. It produces dark brown angular spots on upper leaf surfaces with a dark center and yellow halo.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.pesticide: Mancozeb or Copper oxychloride applied every 10 days during humid periods.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.organic_solution: Neem oil spray. Bordeaux mixture (copper sulfate + lime) as traditional remedy.
prevention: Avoid leaf wetness with drip irrigation

Grape___healthy.description: Grapevine leaves appear healthy with no disease symptoms.
Grape___healthy.organic_solution: Continue regular monitoring and balanced fertilization.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: Huanglongbing (HLB) or citrus greening is the most devastating citrus disease worldwide, caused by the bacterium Candidatus Liberibacter. It causes blotchy mottling of leaves, stunted growth, and bitter, misshapen fruit.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).pesticide: No cure exists. Oxytetracycline injections slow progression temporarily. Control Asian citrus psyllid vector with imidacloprid.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).organic_solution: Remove and destroy infected trees immediately to prevent spread. Control psyllid vector with neem oil spray.
prevention: Control Asian citrus psyllid with insecticides
Plant barrier crops around citrus groves

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of peach is caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni. It causes angular, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown and fall out, causing a shot-hole appearance.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.pesticide: Fixed copper bactericides applied from petal fall through summer. Oxytetracycline (antibiotic) in some regions.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.organic_solution: Copper hydroxide or Bordeaux mixture sprays. Plant resistant varieties.
prevention: Apply copper sprays at bud break

Peach___healthy.description: Peach leaves appear healthy with no disease or pest damage.
prevention: Annual dormant oil spray for scale and mite control

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of pepper is caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria. It causes small, water-soaked spots that enlarge and turn brown with yellow halos on leaves.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.pesticide: Copper-based bactericides (Copper hydroxide) applied every 5–7 days in wet weather.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.organic_solution: Copper sulfate + lime (Bordeaux mixture). Remove infected plants promptly.

Pepper,_bell___healthy.organic_solution: Maintain healthy soil with compost additions.
prevention: Drip irrigation to keep foliage dry

Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight of potato is caused by Alternaria solani. It produces dark, concentric-ringed, target-like lesions on older leaves, starting from the bottom of the plant.
Potato___Early_blight.pesticide: Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, or Azoxystrobin applied every 7–10 days from first signs.
Potato___Early_blight.organic_solution: Copper-based fungicide or Bacillus subtilis-based biocontrol (e.g., Serenade). Neem oil spray.

Potato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans — the organism behind the Irish Potato Famine. It causes large, water-soaked lesions on leaves with white mold on undersides.
Potato___Late_blight.pesticide: Mancozeb, Chlorothalonil, Cymoxanil, or Metalaxyl applied preventively every 5–7 days in wet weather.
Potato___Late_blight.organic_solution: Copper hydroxide or Bordeaux mixture. Remove and destroy infected plant material immediately.
prevention: Hill soil around plants to protect tubers
Monitor weather conditions and apply fungicide before rain
Destroy volunteer plants and solanaceous weeds

Potato___healthy.description: Potato plants appear healthy with vigorous green foliage.
prevention: Maintain balanced potassium and calcium nutrition

Raspberry___healthy.description: Raspberry canes and leaves appear healthy.
Raspberry___healthy.organic_solution: Continue organic mulching and regular pruning.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on squash is caused by Podosphaera xanthii. It appears as white powdery spots on upper and lower leaf surfaces, eventually covering entire leaves.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.pesticide: Potassium bicarbonate, Sulfur, or Trifloxystrobin fungicides. Apply every 7 days.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.organic_solution: Milk spray (40% solution in water — proteins inhibit mildew). Baking soda solution (1 tbsp per gallon). Neem oil.
prevention: Space plants 3–4 feet apart for airflow

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Leaf scorch on strawberry is caused by the fungus Diplocarpon earlianum. It causes small, dark purple to red spots with pale centers on leaves, causing leaf margins to appear scorched.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.pesticide: Captan or Myclobutanil fungicide applied at first sign of infection.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.organic_solution: Copper-based fungicide. Remove infected leaves and plant debris.
prevention: Remove old infected leaves during renovation

Strawberry___healthy.description: Strawberry plants appear healthy with normal green leaves.
Strawberry___healthy.organic_solution: Continue regular monitoring and compost application.
prevention: Scout for pests and disease weekly

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of tomato is caused by Xanthomonas vesicatoria. It causes small, dark water-soaked spots on leaves and fruit, which turn brown and scabby.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.pesticide: Fixed copper bactericide applied every 5–7 days during wet weather. Combine with Mancozeb to reduce resistance.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.organic_solution: Bordeaux mixture (copper sulfate + lime). Avoid working in the garden when plants are wet.
prevention: Remove and destroy infected plant debris

Tomato___Early_blight.description: Tomato early blight is caused by Alternaria solani. Classic symptom is concentric rings forming a target pattern on lower/older leaves, with yellowing around lesions.
Tomato___Early_blight.pesticide: Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, or Azoxystrobin applied every 7–10 days preventively.
Tomato___Early_blight.organic_solution: Neem oil spray every 7 days. Copper fungicide. Bacillus subtilis-based spray (Serenade).
prevention: Mulch around plants to reduce soil splash
Stake/cage plants to keep foliage off ground

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight of tomato (Phytophthora infestans) is a catastrophic disease. Water-soaked lesions rapidly expand, turning brown-black. White mycelial growth visible on undersides in moist conditions.
Tomato___Late_blight.pesticide: Mancozeb, Chlorothalonil, or Metalaxyl/Mefenoxam applied immediately. Spray every 5–7 days during outbreaks.
Tomato___Late_blight.organic_solution: Copper hydroxide or Bordeaux mixture. Remove and bag infected plant material immediately — do not compost.
prevention: Monitor weather forecasts and apply fungicide before rain
Do not plant tomatoes near potatoes
Remove all plant debris at season end

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Tomato leaf mold is caused by Cladosporium fulvum (Fulvia fulva). Yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces with olive-green to grayish mold on the undersides — common in greenhouses.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.pesticide: Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb applied every 7 days. Ensure adequate ventilation.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.organic_solution: Copper-based fungicide. Increase greenhouse ventilation and reduce humidity below 85%.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot is caused by Septoria lycopersici. It produces small, circular spots with dark borders and lighter tan centers. Black dots (pycnidia) visible in spot centers.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.pesticide: Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, or Azoxystrobin applied every 7–10 days from first symptoms.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.organic_solution: Copper fungicide every 7 days. Neem oil. Remove infected lower leaves before applying.
prevention: Mulch under plants to reduce soil splash

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Two-spotted spider mites (Tetranychus urticae) are tiny arachnids causing stippled, bronze discoloration of leaves. Webbing visible under leaves in heavy infestations.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.pesticide: Abamectin, Bifenazate, or Miticide sprays. Rotate classes to prevent resistance.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.organic_solution: Neem oil spray (targets eggs and nymphs). Insecticidal soap spray. Predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) for biological control.
prevention: Maintain adequate soil moisture (drought stress worsens mites)
Keep field free of dusty conditions

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot of tomato is caused by Corynespora cassiicola. Produces distinctive concentric-ring, target-like lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit.
Tomato___Target_Spot.pesticide: Azoxystrobin, Difenoconazole, or Chlorothalonil applied at first sign.
Tomato___Target_Spot.organic_solution: Neem oil or copper-based fungicide. Remove infected plant material.
prevention: Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes dense canopy

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV) is transmitted by the silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). Symptoms include yellowing, upward curling of leaves, stunted growth, and flower drop.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.pesticide: Control whitefly vector with Imidacloprid, Thiamethoxam, or Spiromesifen insecticides. No cure for infected plants.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.organic_solution: Reflective mulch to deter whiteflies. Yellow sticky traps. Neem oil spray on whitefly nymphs. Remove infected plants.
prevention: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV) is mechanically transmitted by tools, hands, and insects. Symptoms include mosaic mottling (light/dark green patches), leaf distortion, and yellow streaking.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.pesticide: No cure. Remove and destroy infected plants. Disinfect tools with 10% bleach solution.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.organic_solution: Wash hands thoroughly before working with plants. Spray milk (20% solution) on tools — proteins inactivate the virus.
prevention: Disinfect tools with bleach/milk between plants

Tomato___healthy.description: Tomato plants are healthy with no signs of disease, pest, or nutritional problems.
Tomato___healthy.organic_solution: Continue regular monitoring. Apply compost for soil health.
